headlines.title: Police investigate Vallejo shooting that injured one person
headlines.description: Vallejo police are investigating a late-night shooting that left one person injured on Monday. The shooting happened shortly before midnight outside a home on Lainey Court, just off Jack London Drive. Police have not released many details about the shooting. Video from the scene showed multiple bullet casings on the ground. According to an NBC Bay Area photographer at the scene, one person was shot and received CPR before being taken from the area. The victim’s condition has not been released. NBC Bay Area has reached out to Vallejo police for more information and will update this story when additional details become available. This is a developing story. Please check back for updates.

headlines.title: Woman fatally stabbed outside Capitola Mall, suspect arrested in random attack
headlines.description: A woman in her 60s was stabbed to death outside the Capitola Mall on Monday afternoon in what police are calling a random act of violence. Capitola Police Department officers responded at about 12:47 p.m. to reports of a stabbing near the mall’s food court exit. They found the victim on Clares Street, across from the west exit of Macy’s, suffering from multiple stab wounds. Officers immediately began life-saving measures until Central Fire personnel and American Medical Response arrived. Despite those efforts, the woman died at the scene. Her identity is being withheld pending the notification of her family. An adult male suspect was later located and taken into custody in the 1400 block of 41st Avenue. Based on the investigation so far, detectives believe the attack was random and that the suspect and victim did not know each other. Police said the stabbing was an isolated incident and there is no ongoing threat to the community. The Capitola Mall was expected to reopen once investigators finished processing the scene. Capitola police are working with the Santa Cruz County Sheriff’s Office Forensic Services Unit, the Santa Cruz County District Attorney’s Office and the Santa Cruz Anti-Crime Team to process the scene and continue the investigation. Because the incident occurred in a public setting, detectives are asking anyone who witnessed the attack or has photographs, video recordings or other information to contact the Capitola PD at 831-475-4242.

headlines.title: Oakland City Council to vote on ‘City of Refuge' ordinance
headlines.description: The Oakland City Council is set to vote Tuesday on an updated “City of Refuge” ordinance aimed at expanding protections for the city’s immigrant community. Oakland became a sanctuary city nearly 40 years ago when city leaders enacted laws in 1986 to protect immigrants from federal enforcement. The proposed ordinance would add new protections. If approved, the ordinance would restrict the collection of immigration status on city applications to keep residents’ information private. The proposal would also prohibit city employees and Oakland police officers from asking about a person’s legal status or sharing that information with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. NBC Bay Area’s Bob Redell has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: San Jose leaders, businesses aim to sustain economic momentum after World Cup
headlines.description: After six weeks of nearly nonstop soccer, life is returning to normal for millions of fans captivated by the World Cup, and South Bay leaders are now strategizing on how to keep the economic momentum and cash flow going. Crews have begun breaking down the setups at San Pedro Square in San Jose, which became a mecca for watch parties during the tournament. The area seemed back to normal for a weekday, with minimal foot traffic, but the last six weeks painted a much different picture. The San Jose Earthquakes initially predicted 350,000 fans would visit San Pedro Square for the entire 39-day tournament. Instead, they lured almost 1 million people. On social media, O’Flaherty’s Irish Pub thanked its many new customers. David Mulvehill, the pub’s owner, expressed his gratitude for the surge in local support. “We’re just very grateful that people came down and supported us. I really want to thank everybody for coming,” Mulvehill said. “I hope they’ll come back, and hopefully we made a good impression, and they’ll come back when there aren’t many activities.” San Jose’s Office of Economic Development has been meeting to see how it can harvest the fruits of the World Cup’s success. The Silicon Valley Chamber of Commerce says it is still crunching the final numbers, but many vow that the region hasn’t seen the end of these large-scale gatherings. Harbir Bhatia, CEO of the Silicon Valley Chamber of Commerce, noted that some businesses are already thinking ahead. For a fee, venues like the domes showed matches on 8K, 360-degree screens, and they plan to replicate that success. “They’re taking the month off and August and working on programming for the football season because now they know people want to come out,” Bhatia said. Business owners like Mulvehill say they want to keep the crowds coming. “Yeah, I think the Super Bowl would be great, but we have some great local teams. The Earthquakes are at the top of the table. It would be nice if they made the playoffs,” Mulvehill said. “We’d have some watch parties down here. Also, the Sharks are looking good.” Bhatia expects this trend to continue well beyond soccer. “I think we’re going to see an uptick in watch parties for every sporting event we have in the Bay Area, and I hope we’ve proven to the world you’ve got to come out to Silicon Valley,” Bhatia said. “We’re much more than just chips.” Tonight, in the now-quiet San Pedro Square, there remains a yearning for more. Hemil Patel, a local sports fan, wants the lively atmosphere to become a permanent fixture. “I think they just should have kept the TVs all year round for all the sporting events,” Patel said. “Especially next year for the FIFA Women’s World Cup.”

headlines.title: 5 teens, 20-year-old arrested in multiple May robberies in San Jose
headlines.description: Five teens and a 20-year-old are facing various charges stemming from a pair of robberies at San Jose businesses in May, police officials announced Monday. Moses Martinez, 20, Frank Maldonado, 19, and four other suspects between the ages of 15 and 17 were all arrested at various times from June 24 to July 2, according to the San Jose Police Department. They are suspected of two robberies, one on May 23 at about 3:35 a.m. at a business in the 2500 block of South King Road and the other on May 26 at about 12:35 a.m. at a business in the 700 block of South Winchester Boulevard. During the first robbery, police say five suspects allegedly entered a business, and one brandished a gun at the victim while four went behind the counter and took boxes of items and then fled in two vehicles. During the second robbery, six suspects, one of whom was allegedly armed, entered a business and took various items before fleeing in two vehicles, police said. Using automatic license plate readers, detectives were able to identify one of the vehicles allegedly used in the crimes, which led them to one of the 15-year-old suspects. He was located on May 26, and a search of his home allegedly uncovered the gun used in the robberies. On June 24, detectives apprehended Martinez and two juvenile suspects in San Jose, and on July 1, with the help of local sheriff’s deputies, a 17-year-old was arrested in Modoc County. Maldonado was arrested on July 2 in Milpitas, according to police officials. The names of the businesses involved were not immediately available. During subsequent searches of the suspects’ homes, detectives allegedly found evidence linking them to the robberies. Anyone with information can contact Detective Ledesma at 4842@sanjoseca.gov or 408-277-4166.

headlines.title: SFMTA to address Fourth of July traffic, transit chaos
headlines.description: San Francisco transit agencies are reviewing transportation problems that followed the city’s Fourth of July fireworks celebration. An estimated 100,000 people traveled to the waterfront to watch fireworks over the Golden Gate Bridge. Many used public transit after city officials encouraged people to leave their cars at home. After the show, riders encountered long waits and crowded trains as they tried to get home. Some said their trips took two to three times longer than expected. “There’s only so much you can do before you just kind of accept your fate,” BART rider Gloria Zhu said. “It was just push and shove into the trains, so we moved with the people and hoped to get on a train before the last one was out.” San Francisco Jul 5 San Francisco Jul 4 NBC Bay Area’s Kris Sanchez has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: Meet the Bay Area artist crafting a national monument to service animals
headlines.description: The pages of history are filled with the achievements of heroes. However, the pages concerning the contributions of the four-legged variety, from horses to dogs, remain a bit skimpy. Marin County sculptor Susan Bahary wants to change that. And her plan to create a large bronze memorial honoring service animals on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., is taking a big stride in Congress. Reps. Jen Kiggans (R-Va.) and Don Davis (D-N.C.) introduced a bill seeking to designate a site on the National Mall for the memorial. Bahary, who leads the National Service Animals Monument group, is excited for the next step. “Hopefully this legislation will get passed and we can get located where we believe we should be, where the animals and people through history should be to tell the American story,” Bahary said in her Novato studio. Bahary’s own creations of bronze figures paying homage to animals occupy pedestals around the world: The World War I Animal Monument in France; The War Dog Memorial in Guam; a tribute to the famous service dog Stubby at the Veteran’s Monument Park in Connecticut, and even a recreation of former President George H.W. Bush’s dog Sully for his presidential library. Artist Susan Bahary creates a dog out of clay inside her Novato studio. Bahary said animals have a long history of rendering aid with no fanfare and little thanks. There were dogs that aided troops in World War I, dolphins trained by the U.S. Navy to patrol coastal areas, dogs that help the blind navigate streets and aid the disabled in navigating their homes. She sees parallels as the nation marks its 250th birthday. “Throughout our history, from George Washington’s horses all the way through to today, we’ve had many kinds of animals that have provided for our safety, security and independence, and alongside the humans have really helped change the course of American history,” Bahary said. Bahary has pursued art since she was a kid. She also loved animals. Eventually she figured out she could combine the two. She began shaping dogs, horses and eventually people out of clay and transforming them into life-sized bronze pieces through foundries. Inside her Novato studio on the Hamilton Field former Air Force base, Bahary works under the gaze of bronze horses, dogs and humans. Her devotion to service animals only grew as she was enlisted to create more monuments honoring the contributions of animals and their human counterparts. The National Service Animals Monument group began 30 years ago, originally known as Critter Lovers at Work. The group streamlined its name and mission, with the idea of creating its namesake memorial on the National Mall, alongside other monuments paying tribute to Vietnam veterans, various presidents and Martin Luther King Jr. Susan Bahary’s mock-up of the National Service Animals Monument on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. In 2022, Congress gave its blessing to the concept of a monument to service animals on the National Mall. Bahary said the next step is designating a site. Bahary, who serves as chair of the board of directors for the group, was chosen to design the monument. Her mock-up shows a number of life-sized bronze animals, including a large horse, a dog searching through debris, another gazing up at a tattered American flag and another guiding a blind man. Bahary said once the site is chosen, the group will need to raise $25 million to pay for the project. She said the project could be ready to go within a few years. The monument currently has the support of various animal organizations. “Throughout history there have been all kinds of service animals, in the military, helping people with disabilities, helping with search and rescue,” said Paige Mazzoni, CEO of Santa Rosa-based Canine Companions, which trains dogs to help veterans and people with disabilities. “They and their handlers are somewhat unsung heroes of great works that have happened through every generation.” Bahary said animals are the unsung heroes of history that have earned a place alongside the other heroes of the two-legged nature. “The animals and people put their lives on the line for the rest of us and they ask very little in return,” Bahary said. “So they do deserve to be recognized.”

headlines.title: Vallejo police fatally shoot carjacking suspect who fired at officers
headlines.description: A man was fatally shot by Vallejo police Monday after officers responded to a report of an alleged armed carjacking, authorities said. According to the Vallejo Police Department, officers were called around 12:19 p.m. to the 400 block of Ryder Street after receiving a 911 call reporting an armed carjacking. Police said officers later located the victim’s vehicle near Mahogany Drive and Broadway Street. When the driver continued onto Mahogany Drive, an officer initiated a felony traffic stop. According to police, the suspect fired at an officer, prompting officers to return fire. Both vehicles were struck by gunfire, and the suspect was hit at least once. Officers rendered medical aid until paramedics arrived, but the suspect was pronounced dead at the scene. Police identified the man as Rommel Delfin Querol, 44, of Vallejo. The two officers involved were not injured and have been placed on administrative leave while criminal and administrative investigations are conducted, police said. The Solano County Major Crimes Task Force has assumed responsibility for the criminal investigation. The City of Vallejo has also retained an independent investigator to conduct an administrative investigation, and the Vallejo Police Department’s Critical Incident Review Board will review the incident, including the department’s tactics, training, supervision, equipment and after-action response. Police said the department plans to hold a town hall meeting within the next seven to 14 days to provide more information about the incident. The investigation remains ongoing. This is a developing story. Stay tuned for updates.

headlines.title: Man critically injured in crash involving off-duty SFPD officer
headlines.description: A man was critically injured Monday morning after he was struck by a vehicle driven by an off-duty San Francisco police officer, according to the San Francisco Police Department. Police said the collision happened at about 7:04 a.m. near Card Alley and Emery Lane. According to SFPD, the off-duty officer was driving a personal vehicle when it struck an adult man in the roadway. Police said the driver immediately stopped and called emergency responders. Additional officers arrived and rendered aid to the man before San Francisco Fire Department personnel arrived. Paramedics transported the man to a hospital, where he was listed in critical condition. The San Francisco Police Department’s Traffic Collision Investigation Unit is leading the investigation. Anyone with information is asked to contact SFPD at 415-575-4444 or text a tip to TIP411, beginning the message with SFPD.

headlines.title: Sausalito leaders to discuss city manager's job after arrest on suspicion of burglary
headlines.description: The Sausalito City Council is scheduled to discuss the job performance of its city manager during a closed session hearing Tuesday following her weekend arrest on suspicion of burglary. According to a statement from city officials, City Manager Elaine Forbes was on a leave of absence from her job when she was arrested by the Marin County Sheriff’s Office on Saturday morning. “This is a difficult development for our City staff and our community,” according to a statement posted on the city’s website and on social media. “It is anticipated the City Council will discuss next steps at its upcoming City Council meeting.” According to agenda items posted online, the 3 p.m. closed session includes a “public employee performance evaluation” and the “public employment” of the city manager’s position. The session is closed to the public and it’s unclear if Forbes’ arrest will be discussed during the City Council’s public meeting right after. Forbes, 53, was arrested Saturday morning at 501 Humboldt Ave., which is the address of the Sausalito Yacht Harbor, according to Marin County Jail records. She was booked on suspicion of felony burglary and is being held on $50,000 bail. The City Council placed Forbes on leave during a closed session hearing on Thursday and Brandon Phipps is now serving as acting city manager, according to city officials. A spokesperson for the Forbes family, Sam Singer, sent out a statement on Sunday asking people not to rush to judgement. “Elaine is experiencing a mental health crisis,” wrote Singer. “As the facts become clearer and Elaine obtains legal and healthcare counsel in the coming hours and days, we hope people will respond with compassion, patience and respect for her and her family during an extraordinarily difficult time.” Forbes began her role as city manager on July 1 after being appointed by the City Council. Her experience included nine years as director of the Port of San Francisco, where she oversaw waterfront infrastructure, maritime operations, public access and real estate management. “Elaine Forbes has devoted much of her life to public service and is widely respected by the many people who have worked with her over the years,” said Singer. City officials directed inquires to the Marin County Sheriff’s Office, which didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment Monday afternoon.
headlines.source: Kiley Russell and Katy St. Clair | Bay City News

headlines.title: 1 dead in shooting near Oakland's Lake Merritt
headlines.description: Oakland police are investigating a shooting death from early Sunday near Lake Merritt. Just before 3 a.m., officers were sent to the 1400 block of Lakeside Drive, near the Lake Chalet restaurant, in response to multiple alerts from the city’s automatic gunfire detection system. When they arrived, officers found a man who had been shot at least once and died at the scene, police said Monday. No arrests were announced and the man’s name wasn’t released. Anyone with information can call the Oakland Police Department’s Homicide Section at (510) 238-3821 or the department’s tip line at (510) 238-7950. Photos and videos can be sent to cidvideos@oaklandca.gov.

headlines.title: Residents protest planned data center at Pittsburg City Hall in Contra Costa County
headlines.description: A fight over a planned data center brought dozens of people to City Hall, where residents demanded city leaders hit the pause button on the project. About 80 people gathered outside before the City Council meeting to protest the facility, which was approved last year. Many residents said they only learned about the project within the past week and expressed concerns over its potential impacts on power demand and water use. Amhari Perkins, who started an online petition to stop the project, was among the demonstrators. “I’m literally talking to people out here who just found out a week ago,” Perkins said. “They just found out a week ago, you know?” Resident Eder Garcia echoed the frustration over the lack of communication. “You should have involved our residents more,” Garcia said. “We’re the ones who have to live with a data center.” Avaio Digital is building the data center as part of Pittsburg’s planned Technology Park at the former Delta View Golf Course. City leaders argue the project will create jobs, generate tax revenue and help transform the site into a technology hub. Opponents, however, worry the costs could outweigh the benefits. “The effects it will have on power. I’ve heard concerns about water usage and humming noise, but for me, it’s power,” Perkins said. Those concerns quickly filled the council chambers, even though the data center was not a formal agenda item. Extra seating was set up outside the chambers to accommodate the crowd. Before public comment began, the mayor called for a civil discussion. “I want to remind everyone we welcome all viewpoints and respectful disagreement,” the mayor said. During public comment, speaker after speaker urged the city to slow down, demanding more answers before the project moves forward. “It should be clear to you by now that a mistake has been made,” one public commenter said. There were some voices in support of the facility, including one speaker who emphasized the project’s potential to bring employment opportunities to Pittsburg. “There won’t be any good jobs locally. Everyone will be going over the hill,” the commenter said. The city stated the project has already completed its environmental review. According to officials, the facility will be air-cooled, use mostly recycled water for optional pre-cooling and the data center operator will pay its own energy costs. Data centers have faced similar pushback in other Bay Area cities, including Gilroy and Oakley. But back in Pittsburg, residents say the heavy turnout should send a clear message to leaders. “I think a lot of residents are emotional about it, and we’re just not receiving answers,” Perkins said. The mayor announced that the next City Council meeting on Aug. 3 will focus entirely on data centers. Meanwhile, city officials said the developer has not yet announced when construction will begin.
